stage.name: Wallets & Safety (Elementary)
stage.description: What crypto wallets are, how keys work, and how to stay safe.

elements.content: 👛 What is a Crypto Wallet?
Your key to your crypto — not a physical wallet

A crypto wallet doesn't actually store your coins — your coins live on the blockchain. What a wallet stores are your secret keys — the passwords that prove the coins belong to you. Think of it like a mailbox key. Your crypto is in the mailbox (blockchain). The key proves it's yours. Lose the key — someone else opens your mailbox.

🔑

Public Key

Like your address — safe to share

🤫

Private Key

Like your house key — NEVER share

🔥

Hot Wallet

Connected to internet — easy to use

🧊

Cold Wallet

Offline — safest for large amounts

elements.content: 📝 The Seed Phrase
The master key to your entire wallet

When you set up a wallet, you get a seed phrase — usually 12 or 24 random words like: apple river cloud sunset forest key... These words can restore your entire wallet if your phone breaks. But they're also dangerous — anyone with your seed phrase owns your wallet.

✅

Write on Paper

Store it somewhere safe

❌

No Screenshots

Never photograph it

❌

Never Share

Not with anyone — ever

✅

Multiple Copies

Store in separate safe places

elements.content: 🏦 Custodial vs Non-Custodial
Who actually controls your crypto?

Custodial wallet — a company (like Coinbase or Binance) holds your private keys. You log in with a username and password. Easy and convenient, but they control your money. If they get hacked or go bankrupt, your funds could be lost.


Non-custodial wallet — you hold your own private keys. Nobody can freeze or take your funds. This is true crypto ownership. The phrase in crypto is: "Not your keys, not your coins."

🏦 Custodial
Exchange holds keys — convenient

🔑 Non-Custodial
You hold keys — true ownership

⚠️ CEX Risk
Exchange can fail or freeze

✅ Best Practice
Move large amounts off exchanges

actions.text: 🏦 Custodial vs Non-Custodial
Who actually controls your crypto?

Custodial wallet — a company (like Coinbase or Binance) holds your private keys. You log in with a username and password. Easy and convenient, but they control your money. If they get hacked or go bankrupt, your funds could be lost. Non-custodial wallet — you hold your own private keys. Nobody can freeze or take your funds. This is true crypto ownership. The phrase in crypto is: "Not your keys, not your coins."
🏦 Custodial. Exchange holds keys — convenient
🔑 Non-Custodial. You hold keys — true ownership
⚠️ CEX Risk. Exchange can fail or freeze
✅ Best Practice. Move large amounts off exchanges

elements.content: 🌱 Seed Phrases — The Master Key
The 12 or 24 words that control everything

When you create a non-custodial wallet, you get a seed phrase — 12 or 24 random words in a specific order. These words are your master key. They can regenerate your entire wallet on any device, anywhere in the world.


The rules: Write it on paper (not digital). Store it somewhere safe and private. Never share it with anyone. Never type it into a website. Anyone who has your seed phrase owns all your crypto.

📝 Write on Paper
Never store digitally

🔒 Store Safely
Fireproof location is best

🚫 Never Share
Not with support, not with anyone

♻️ Recovery
Restores wallet on any device

scenes.title: 🛡️ How Not to Lose Your Funds

elements.content: 🛡️ How Not to Lose Your Funds
The most important safety habits in crypto
Most crypto losses are not from hacks — they are from human mistakes. Here are the top ways people lose funds and how to avoid them.

📤 Wrong Address
Always double-check before sending

🌱 Lost Seed Phrase
Back it up in multiple safe places

🎣 Phishing
Never click wallet-connect links in DMs

📱 Phone Lost
Use hardware wallet for large amounts

content.html:
🔐 Wallet Builder

Walk through setting up a crypto wallet — generate an address, reveal a seed phrase, and test your security knowledge.

actions.text: Let's explore this interactively. Use the simulation below to experiment with the concepts we just covered.
Try different inputs and see how the outputs change. Hands-on exploration builds intuition faster than reading.

questions.question: What does a crypto wallet actually store?

options.label: Your secret keys that prove ownership of your coins
A copy of the entire blockchain

questions.analysis: Your coins live on the blockchain — the wallet stores your private keys, which prove you own those coins. Whoever has the keys, owns the coins.

questions.question: What is your public key like?

options.label: Your house key — never share it
Your home address — safe to share so people can send you crypto

questions.analysis: Your public key is like your address. Share it with anyone who wants to send you crypto. It's completely safe to share.

questions.question: Which of these is the MOST dangerous thing to do with your seed phrase?

options.label: Write it on paper and lock it in a safe
Make two copies stored in separate locations
Type it into a website that claims to 'verify' your wallet

questions.analysis: Typing your seed phrase into ANY website is one of the fastest ways to lose all your crypto. Scammers build fake sites specifically to steal seed phrases.

questions.question: What is the difference between a hot wallet and a cold wallet?

options.label: Hot wallets are better for large savings, cold wallets for daily spending
Hot wallets are connected to the internet (easier but riskier), cold wallets are offline (safer for large amounts)
Cold wallets are illegal in most countries

questions.analysis: Hot wallets stay connected to the internet — easy to use but more vulnerable. Cold wallets are offline devices like hardware wallets — harder to hack but less convenient for daily use.

actions.text: Time to test what you've learned! Answer each question and see how you do.
Take your time — the goal is understanding, not just getting the right answer.
